sentences.text: And Josef, this is a-- Andre, he was a [NON-ENGLISH], he and what a Russian worker.
The Russian give the [NON-ENGLISH], and the daughter, Ludia, those three.
When they-- [NON-ENGLISH] is the boss, the lady, the boss.
When she sees, this is the right guy which is looking for him.
I start to make that leather, which she does-- she put them sew-- whatever she does, she doesn't know what she's doing.
Then in middle of the night, about 12 o'clock at night, only in the daytime I was hided, I sharp the knife-- we call it [NON-ENGLISH].
And I-- she is-- I start to make that leather.
Yells.
Gives a-- gives a whistle, because if she sharps the knife, gives a whistle.
And she sees the leather gets beautiful.
And I told her what to do.
She buy this and this and this.
And I worked that leather, and make the leather finish it.
And she goes to the big city, Luck-- L-U-C-K, Luck.
She goes to sell this leather, and she goes now to sell it to make sure.
And a guy said, a shoemaker says, Andre this leather is-- somebody is Jewish and he make that.
That is fresh and brilliant work.
They look, and the Nazis looking for a-- a man like this.
She says, I tell you what, I am like-- it was two months I make that leather.
Where did you buy?
He says, OK.
I'll go home.
Maybe I'll-- then meanwhile, he makes a pair of shoes.
I make that leather.
And she says to me, I don't know.
I went, two months later.
He disappeared.
And I can't find him anymore.
But I have a, a little bit soles, I'll give it-- sell it to you.
A pair of soles, you could have a bag of under a kilo tomatoes, [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
I don't know what to tell you more with that story, about Josef.
Then, they gave us-- they've been [NON-ENGLISH].
At night, they call us [NON-ENGLISH] in the Russian language.
The daughter comes, Ludia, later Andre come.
Now December, December, the holidays.
They bring us out food.
And she says to me, I couldn't understand.
We got so much to eggs, and you never touch eggs.
Because the chickens are laying the eggs, and I never touch eggs, because why should I.
Then I will give you 5-6 eggs, because you never touch it, the eggs.
She knows how much eggs, how much chicken, you know, that way.
I didn't know about it.
But I wouldn't touch the egg.
And she give us to eat.
December we had a good meal.
December and January-- January, I fixed the leather-- the leather.
And then March comes friends all from my town.
Knocks on the door, Jews.
He says, he's Jewish.
I want to have a piece of bread.
And they give him a piece of bread.
And they ask about, us, whether that they are there.
He goes, we are-- he was in the forest, and we don't know where he is.
She says to him, he was in the-- we've been in the [NON-ENGLISH].
He said, if you see him, you tell him that I'm looking for him.
She says to this guys, she gives him the-- she gives him bread, she gives him this-- she gives him whatever he want.
And they're going away, and then she comes in, and she says, come the next-- tomorrow, I'll give you again.
I'm coming in the next day, and she says to me, they're coming.
You want to see them?
I went the other side, I'm coming in.
And they've been already there.
Who's this?
Jews.
Who is this?
Jews.
Oh, come on in.
They come on in, we see each other from my town.
We see each other, and they're telling me we have hided there, three girls there, and we are six, seven men in a hole.
In the daytime, we're going away in the forest down there.
At night, we come at 12:00 or 1 o'clock, we cook potatoes, and we cook, and we making fire.
In the daytime, we go away.
Telling her this story, then we go back.
We didn't go there.
He says, we're going just to look at it.
I went overnight to the forest with them.
March '44, March '44-- no.
No, no, that was February-- that was January, I think, January or March, something like this.
January or March.
February or something like this.
Or was it February?
In February, I went to-- then I go outside to ride in the daytime.
Because the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the prisoner, which is the prisoner, they call it.
He told me, they organized-- they call us to the army.
They make a new Ukrainish army.
We told him not to say anything about us.
He didn't say anything.
Once or twice, we're walk or ride in the morning, stop.
They were going to shoot us.
Who's there?
I says, I say, I'm the tanner that make the leather.
Oh, we know you.
You go yourself.
They let us go.
They let us go.
We know they looking for us.
About-- in, in May-- Well, in March is when you went to the forest and joined the partisans, right?
No, March, something like this.
But then from March, not no-- I didn't join the partisans.
I'm still there.
I'm still there.
But in May 1st or May 3rd, they make a killing for the Polacks.
They kill all the Polacks.
They kill all the Polacks.
It was in May.
The Ukrainish, the Bandera.
He kills all the Polacks.
And I was there also.
Daytime, I was with my Polack, making leather.
And they took us out near the forest-- in the-- in the forest.
And they see 15, 8, 10-- and one guy, which he was with me, says I see the a [INAUDIBLE] they kill.
They took a stick, and they hit them right here.
Not with bullets.
Bullets are money.
They hit him right here, and they fall in the grave.
And then he says to me, you sit over here.
We are-- nobody watches you, but don't go away, in the forest.
Planning?
Where are we going to go?
They already won.
Something is there, a new-- we didn't know about the army.
We didn't know about these things.
We sitting, sitting at night at 12 o'clock at night. 12 o'clock at night, they took us on the other side, in the main office in [NON-ENGLISH].
The Banderas organized with a safe.
They have a safe, they have money.
They have everything down there.
Because 12 o'clock [INAUDIBLE] because the-- the road is already Germans, and the Germans go with the machine up front to back.
About 12 o'clock, no German goes through.
They have two times attacked the Germans.
And they took the machines in.
It was late, then we, we go away, about six, seven wagons.
On the other side we come in [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is the village name.
And they put us on, and he says, listen to me.
We give us all the clothes that belongs to you, everything belongs-- we're the bosses.
And you organize the leather fact-- the leather.
I take this leather which I find with the Polacks and Ukrainish, all the leathers belongs to them.
And you organize, and we make our factory from leather.
We put out all, and I-- now go, find workers.
I say go to the-- they have workers, the Poland the Ukrainian, they have everything.
They say we've got liberated-- everybody is liberated.
Only come to us.
And I find Jews.
And we're working.
They're digging down a big hole, and putting up to make that leather-- boots, you know, belts.
And I start to make that leather, and we have two horses.
And we grind the, the wood.
We have to be grind it like powder.
And we make that leather.
And I make that leather.
And the leather which I make it, then I go to the city.
I could go to the Lwow and be free.
I had a-- a name, a different name, a different passport, [NON-ENGLISH].
I could go to-- to Lwow, a big city.
I didn't go.
I didn't go.
I changed my name.
But I go-- I go to Luck, the big city.
We had lunches down there.
And we look for material.
In Luck I look-- I look in the restaurant, [NON-ENGLISH] 28, the street, [NON-ENGLISH], in Luck.
I see the restaurant.
And the guy which is with me-- go with another guy with me-- he says to me, you not go in the front.
Five Jews they caught hided, and they-- they put them to, to, to kill them.
You know that.
I could eat, I says.
The other guy says, why don't you tell him that story.
You couldn't eat break-- lunch or something like this.
That's what I see. 1943, I was with the Bandera.
The partisans, later.
I was liberated.
After the liberation, I go to the partisans.
After I got liberated It was-- it was worse than ever, after I got liberated.
OK.
Liberated.
Liberated in March 15, 1944, is liberated already.
How I got liberated, I then tell you now.
We went in with the partisans.
They come at night.
We don't know who they are.
They come in Luck.
We go in Luck.
They arrested us, and they checked us.
And they see, he says we didn't work.
I didn't-- I shouldn't-- I shouldn't say that.
But they forgive me.
I don't know they were Bandera.
Because I was different dressed than all of them.
I wore good shoes, you know.
I told them that.
He says, next time, to captain, don't say anything.
Because this is no good for you to work for them.
After then, we go on Rovno free, Rovno free.
And suddenly I go with two girls with Israel, Hamka Stryker and [NON-ENGLISH].
And go in there, no-- the other guys.
In the back with me, comes a guy.
Who's this?
Dr.
Ehrluf from my town.
We were raised together as childhood.
He remembers me.
He comes in, come on to me.
I come into him-- in his office.
And in office, about 10-20 people waiting for him.
He says, I'll talk to you.
He give me cream.
He give me this, he give me and the girls.
And he says, come later, and we go for lunch.
Well, you got plenty to do, do you?
He said, I have plenty to do in the whole world.
You cannot be anymore over here, because [NON-ENGLISH] is danger.
I feel danger.
What are you going to do?
He said, I belong to the party, the Communist Party.
And I am protected.
I have this, I have that.
You was going outside.
And I start to work for the factory in there, Rovno.
Then this night I went away to another town, and over there-- six Jewish people working only in that factory.
And they called-- and they took out all the Jewish, six Jewish, and they killed them.
I see no life.
And they're looking at all of us.
I'm Jewish.
But the Russian-- the Russian army already.
This is no life.
I went to them, I says, listen.
I don't want to work.
It's time to fight with the Nazis.
So I says, maybe yes.
But you speak Polish, you speak Russian, you speak Ukrainish.
They send me away to Lublin-- no, to the forest.
I come to the same place, which I did work, not to same town, just different.
In the night we worked, in the daytime we went to the forest to hide ourselves.
It was about 12, 13 people.
Till we pull up so to Chelm, Chelm.
And in Chelm, we went to the forest.
And with Chelm, they went away-- I told you-- what's his name-- in the forest, everything in the forest.
And they have-- and they have food from-- from the Polacks, and meals from there.
And suddenly they went away.
After Chelm, we went away outside, three miles, I told you before.
And bring food from the-- from the village.
And we bring every day, going one day, second day, and then they caught us.
The Nazis caught us in the city-- I mark it down, the city-- I mark it down.
You remember?
They caught us, and they bring us to Lublin, in the ghetto.
In Lublin ghetto, they give us Juden [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish soap, J-U-S-- R-J-U-S, Reich Juden [NON-ENGLISH], to soap up, in clothes and everything else.
And the other clothes, they went away.
And they start checking the [NON-ENGLISH].
One guy checked the hands.
They said, you have clean hands, and strong, really strong.
And everything is nice.
And maybe they'll do something with me.
And I said to the man, the man I do this, I do this.
They send me here.
They send me there.
They send me to kitchen.
I fix the table.
Suddenly, there were-- there was glass-- I was a glass cutter.
You know, I find it out.
I cut glass.
He says, he does everything.
Keep.
And they kept me down there.
But half a day's work, sometimes I have to take dead people.
It's terrible.
I didn't like it.
But the beginning, when all-- in every-- in every place you have dead people, three, four, five, six.
And the dead people, go to the oven.
And the oven goes like this in Lublin, oven like a chain goes.
You see loaded on material, the same thing.
Loaded on material, wood goes up.
I only have to [INAUDIBLE].
When I see a friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH], laying dead.
Not dead, but I didn't take him.
I recognized.
He couldn't talk.
Then I meet another guy, which I-- and years later-- a day after liberation I meet him, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I don't know, I hadn't heard from him.
Because a lot, you die after this.
A lot of people died.
In Majdanek.
This is in Majdanek.
They took me to Majdanek.
It was--
May-- yes, May 15th, early, something like this.
And I was there.
I do the same thing.
In June--
Working together with them.
And then the factory there which they need me to work, they says, this guy in the factory, the Lublin factory.
They need me to work.
He says, I can't give out this guy.
Because I need him myself.
He's a guy [INAUDIBLE].
And I would work in the kitchen a little bit.
I peel potatoes-- I can peel potatoes, you know, for the-- for the commissar, not for the-- for the commissar, peel potatoes and cutting meat, and sharpen knives, all of that.
They kept me.
We need him.
Suddenly I see-- in June 12 or 13, nobody.
The doors is open, big doors from the ghetto, open.
And the second, the third day, and so by 13, I think-- I says, what the day.
And I have-- is there a-- a place with clothes there, a lot of clothes, like two bags up, way up clothes.
And I always had with me in the pants in my pocket, an undershirt with me.
And I walk out from the ghetto-- from the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was 5:30, 6 o'clock, sometime, I didn't have a watch.
And I walk out across the street, [NON-ENGLISH], now [NON-ENGLISH] is an outside city toilet.
The toilet goes on here 2, 3, 4, and the other side for ladies, 2, 3, 4.
And I go in there.
I sit a half an hour, an hour.
They knock on the door.
They knock on the door.
I says, no place.
And I went in-- into there.
And I hide myself in a post, and I got all this stuff in around me, all the, the, the worms crawl on me.
You see, I knew-- I knew-- I figured this is the place to hide myself to live.
By my friends at [NON-ENGLISH], I says, more patience and more to-- you could have chances to live over.
And I figured the same thing.
And suddenly about 11 o'clock at night, I go out and close the toilet.
Nobody goes in, and stayed there, till 12 o'clock. 12 o'clock I took off my clothes, [NON-ENGLISH], and a shirt, it was a shirt.
A uniform, [INAUDIBLE] uniform.
And I run away, [NON-ENGLISH] on the side, in the side, along on the side in the village, and run away a couple of miles, eight miles.
And the Germans was already 5 o'clock, 6:00 in the morning.
And Germans bombed the ones in the house, some other people also, regular, like other, any other.
And they was bombed.
And I was injured, some people dead.
And I was injured over here in the back.
And I can't talk.
Four broken ribs I had.
And they call-- it was already a day, Piaski was liberated.
The city was liberated, and Lublin probably also was liberated.
I didn't know.
So eight miles.
And they see I'm Jewish.
And they, they call up Jewish people there living already in the city, in [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish people in the Piaski.
In that city, small town.
And they come and go out, and they took me away, with a wagon, four wheels, like the dead people there, they have a wagon, you know, four wheels.
And they took me in, and [NON-ENGLISH] says, you know, you're liberated.
Don't worry about it.
We're already liberated.
We have Russians over here.
And there was a Jewish captain, a Jewish mayor in the city.
I remember his name.
You'd never think he Jewish.
And they gave me a place to live to-- straw.
And the grandmother, which is my father-in-law's mother, gave me cream and bread, and I eat the bread and talk.
I can't talk.
He can't walk.
He can't talk.
And my wife, she's already [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't know that.
She comes, I remember she put her hand in over here.
She was beautiful.
Yeah.
I didn't know her.
[LAUGHS] She puts her hand.
She says, he has no temperature.
She says, I don't see any temperature.
He called a doctor [NON-ENGLISH].
He was in the city, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know.
Doctor [NON-ENGLISH] says, he will need a strong guy.
He's terrible injured four-- four ribs.
And it was six weeks.
I start to walk with a cane.
And now, from my town, a city-- from my town passed through down there.
And he comes in.
A Jew is down there.
He comes to me.
He see me.
Oy, he says to me my name.
And I can't talk to him.
He says, you know who is this.
This guy which went in this, in this, and this.
I went with him here.
We know each other for a long time.
He tell the story.
Then they put together a bed for me from boards, two boards, straw.
And I was laying in bed.
And I was about two months probably.
It was Passover.
I try-- I work hard.
I work hard.
I start to talk Russian.
I bring in a Russian guy with-- I sell-- I already bought leather, sell leather.
I made a coat.
Leather, no I bought leather.
And the leather, I went to sell it.
And the Russian guy comes to me, I have $100 hundred American. $100 American, $100 American.
No not American-- zlotys.
No dollars.
[LAUGHS]
Hundred [NON-ENGLISH] This is Russian.
He's Russian.
And I got 100, this one, I got Polish.
Could you give me that leather?
Five-- skin leather, he goes, yeah.
But this at 500 ruble.
I says, I got only 300.
I say, well, OK.
Give me, [LAUGHS] and I took away.
I made me 200 dollars right away.
But I had over here, five, six, golden [NON-ENGLISH], golden Russians.
And for this case, I gave away to my wife before, before.
I says, I'm satisfied.
I have this and this.
Two pieces I give you away.
My father-in-law, I can't take it.
He don't take mine was damaged.
And he gave me away good ones, which is double price.
Then she make two teeths over here.
I remember.
He don't want to accept anything from me.
And I was there.
And I says this way, see we have [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], the president from the-- from the city, and they the police, and Jewish police.
She says, I don't believe it.
We have a war.
I don't believe in anything.
After the war, you have to be Jewish two, three years to be recognized as a human being.
Right now is no good.
I said, I don't want to live here.
I want to go to a big town, Lublin.
In Lublin I went two months.
And then they went back, and they kill all of them-- [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], all the Jews, the president, they kill all of them, the, the bandit.
The cop, Polish army [NON-ENGLISH] they call it.
They kill all of them.
He says-- my father-in-law sees I'm right.
Then I says, not place to live here.
And I went away, and made a big business in Lodz.
From Lodz I went to Cracow.
I bring a lot flour here, a lot of flour there.
Oh, my father was died right away in September.
It was, my father and my mother was at the same time.
It was a different time.
Look, he was in, in 1942, in the holidays, they kill-- all the area was killed.
But our town, Lemberg, was later.
My father was a week later.
Just [INAUDIBLE].
Let's just forget about it.
This is no question.
I know that.
Yeah, then I went-- you have SS, I don't want to stay over here with that business in Lublin.
And I went away.
Then here, the father came in September, was the father came.
He says, I want to stay with-- my wife came in middle of the day.
It was in July or August.
She came.
I want to have with you-- I want to be with you.
Sister and father says no.
And is there a rabbi, which is there a rabbi in Brooklyn, rabbi-- he's there in Brooklyn.
And the rabbi likes to get with the-- with the-- with the father-in-law, he say, to give permission.
I says, I want to get married with your daughter.
And another guy comes.
And she says, she likes me.
The lady, which she comes over here, I want to bring her up.
You want to-- and she's going to tell you the story.
She knows-- she remembers better.
Because my wife told her the story.
And she came to Lodz, and she said-- and to the business, the store which we had leather.
I was busy.
And I had in the store three people working for me, two partners and the rest people in Lodz.
And I comes-- and I work in the factory.
And I'm not-- and a stranger.
I make $20 American money a day, the long dollars, one day.
And she came, not as a businessman.
She came because she had been with you.
And I helped you to get well.
I want to be with you.
I had a girl.
She's living.
She's in Israel.
She works for me.
And I figure this way, by myself and I got sick.
Every, every April I have a throat problem.
In this time also, [NON-ENGLISH], she's in Israel.
And I give her permission.
I say, [NON-ENGLISH], I tell you what.
I'm decided.
I'm by myself.
And I have money, I have dollars, [BACKROUND NOISE] and I have [INAUDIBLE].
Let me get married, and be my wife.
She says, I tell you what.
I like to accept your, your invitation.
But I have-- see the guy comes, whatever.
He's a doctor.
He's in the hospital.
And he says, we want to get married.
You know?
He has a hospital, and he comes over here.
We're going [NON-ENGLISH], half an hour.
And I go for two hours.
Sometimes she don't say anything.
Because I know that.
OK.
I give up.
I gave up the whole thing.
Now wait, another 2-3 weeks, she comes-- she comes over here suddenly in the daytime.
She comes over here.
And she says to me, I-- like you, this and this, and this and this.
And I come over and talk to my partners, and I talk to my business people.
I, I, I received another letter, and I talked it over with them.
And they said the same thing.
This is going to be your wife.
And the other one, she has him.
She doesn't go with you, she go with others.
But this is a good business lady.
She said, oh, we need [INAUDIBLE] the business.
So she signed.
She knows I've got a letter.
She said, I don't need that stuff.
I tossed her with money, with cash, with everything else.
She say, I never leave it.
She says, I don't like to see that.
And this lady will tell you the story, which I tried.
You want her to come, she come right away over here.
And she goes away, and the other one comes.
She says stay over here.
But I don't want to stay without [NON-ENGLISH] It was still Friday, Thursday, she called up the father.
Not so easy to call up.
You have to go a telephone.
She called her father.
Her father comes Friday, and stays over Saturday.
He brings me 5 [NON-ENGLISH], about American $500 or $400, Russian money.
He said, this is your dowry.
And this I go-- I didn't ask him.
He gave me.
He says, OK.
Now I says, I don't stay over here.
I want to go.
The world, I don't want to stay here.
I says, you go back go home with your father.
No.
I go.
I stay with you.
And I went right away, September I went to Germany, September 5.
September, September, the month of September.
I went to Germany.
I came to Germany in the Russian zone.
And over there, I'm-- a few of the Jewish survivors there, Russians there.
And looking for a job, I remember, they gave me a job to do.
And she's pregnant already.
She became pregnant, yeah.
And I say it's not a place between the war-- I've heard a lot of stories about this.
And I says I go to American zone, and go up to Hanover.
And I recognize and I speak to the main man, Yosel Rosensaft.
He's there.
He's still living, Yosel Rosensaft.
And by a castle, Hanover.
And I says to him, and he said to me, listen what had happened.
You know, you take out your card, your registration card.
Because you are liberated in 1944.
And don't say anything.
I says, I wouldn't say-- I want to have the truth, the way it is, open.
Comes anything to me is OK.
He says I'd rather want you coming away from there.
They say they are Russian.
The take out their index cards.
And I don't want to take out anything.
I want the truth.
And I was there a couple of months in Germany.
Yosel Rosensaft to speak with a lager-- I lost $300 American, a big story.
I lost $300 over here.
I lost it.
You know.
I had money.
I make business, you know the way it is.
My wife used to say, you lost the money. $300 is a lot of money, but what?
Now the lady says, somebody find the $300 there.
I know.
It's a big story, complicated.
And I go-- from there, I go to Frankfort.
Yeah.
